alt="DSC05915.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1d09bb5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4898x2749+0+258/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F43%2F7f%2Fddf782004f4abe6293156eaee8eb%2Fdsc05915.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9524975/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4898x2749+0+258/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F43%2F7f%2Fddf782004f4abe6293156eaee8eb%2Fdsc05915.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1d09bb5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4898x2749+0+258/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F43%2F7f%2Fddf782004f4abe6293156eaee8eb%2Fdsc05915.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Singer and self-proclaimed “history nerd” Lou Ella Rose Cabalona has spent several years researching Filipino composers and their ties to Chicago. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Lou Ella Rose Cabalona</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h2>Exploring ‘The Great Filipino Songbook’</h2><p>After years of researching Filipino music, self-proclaimed history nerd Lou Ella Rose Cabalona has made a delightful discovery.</p><p>“There is a profound connection between Chicago and the Philippines when it comes to music,” said the Filipino American singer and Norwood Park resident.</p><p>That link was formed when influential Filipino composers Francisco Santiago and Nicanor Edelardo migrated to the city in the 1920s and 1930s, respectively. Both studied at the Chicago Musical College, which is now known as Roosevelt University’s Chicago College of Performing Arts. The men were pioneers of the kundiman, a genre of traditional Filipino love songs that also doubled as patriotic odes amid colonial rule.</p><p>Cabalona will expose local audiences to the musical style while telling cultural stories during “<a class="Link" href="https://www.choosechicago.com/event/lou-ella-rose-and-friends-the-great-filipino-songbook/" target="_blank" ><u>The Great Filipino Songbook</u></a>” on May 15 at the Epiphany Center for the Arts.</p><p>“It's heart-wrenching,” she said of the music. “It's sad. It's always about unrequited love. And unrequited love can also be parallel to longing for freedom and independence of a people.”</p><p>Accompanied by guest artists and her band, the SamaSama Project, Cabalona will also perform folk songs in Filipino languages. She said she is excited to share her heritage with the audience.</p><p>“I think the cabaret stage is perfect for that, because it is very welcoming of all genres, styles and kinds of people,” she said.</p><p>Before moving to the U.S. to pursue a career in IT, Cabalona was a musician and musical theater performer in the Philippines. Though she was exposed to traditional Filipino music, her recent studies have deepened her understanding of its origins and development. She even received a fellowship from the Asian Cultural Council to conduct research in the country last summer.</p><p>“I feel like I have to have that foundation as an immigrant musician,” she said. “That’s where I get my inspiration for my artistry.”</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-230000" name="module-230000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><b>May 15:</b> “<a class="Link" href="https://www.choosechicago.com/event/lou-ella-rose-and-friends-the-great-filipino-songbook/" target="_blank" >Lou Ella Rose and Friends — The Great Filipino Songbook</a>": 7 p.m. at the Epiphany Center for the Arts, 201 S. Ashland Ave., $28.</div> </div><p></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-360000" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Ava Logan will pay homage to jazz singers Josephine Baker, Bricktop and Ella Fitzgerald during Chicago Cabaret Week.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Ava Logan </p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h2>Honoring jazz legends, from Josephine Baker to ‘Bricktop’</h2><p>Like Cabalona, singer Ava Logan also traced the history of her musical influences. Last year, she traveled to France with Working in Concert’s cultural exchange program, Chicago Paris Cabaret Connexion. In Dordogne, she visited the chateau of iconic Black performer Josephine Baker.</p><p>“It was so emotional for me,” said Logan, of Orland Park, who is a veterinarian by day and a performer by night. She said she was inspired by Baker’s decision to move and build a life abroad at a young age.</p><p>On May 16 at Haven Entertainment Center, Logan will perform Baker’s signature song, “J'ai deux amours,” which is about her “two loves” — the U.S. and Paris. Titled “<a class="Link" href="https://www.choosechicago.com/event/jazz-zing-with-ava-logan/" target="_blank" ><u>Jazz Zing!</u></a>,” Logan’s show will also feature selections by Ella Fitzgerald, Alberta Hunter, Sarah Vaughan, Nancy Wilson and Dinah Washington.</p><p>“I want to highlight some of our pioneers who got us all started in jazz,” Logan said.</p><p>That list also includes singer Ada “Bricktop” Smith, another U.S. expat who grew up in Chicago and moved to Paris, where she operated several clubs. Logan will sing a rendition of Bricktop’s saucy “Insufficient Sweetie.”</p><p>“I don't get an opportunity to perform things like that, so I'm really excited to do it,” she said.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/music/2024/12/20/chicago-cabaret-group-black-voices-in-cabaret-makes-amplifying-black-voices-its-mission" >Chicago’s Black Voices in Cabaret makes amplifying Black artists its mission</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Logan said her background in drama will come in handy for the performance; the D.C. native acted in theater productions in high school and later portrayed Fitzgerald in “Ella: The First Lady of Song” at the Black Ensemble Theater. She will also sing a number from that show, “Why Was I Born?" at the Haven, one of many <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/arts-and-culture/2026/04/26/for-international-jazz-day-residents-walk-paths-of-nat-king-cole-louis-armstrong-in-bronzeville" target="_blank" ><u>historic jazz venues in the neighborhood</u></a>.</p><p>“It’s very inspiring to perform in Bronzeville,” she said. “It’s an amazing history we have here in Chicago.”</p><p>Logan said she hopes Chicago Cabaret Week will bring some “healing” to audiences.</p><p>“We definitely need to have a fun side to our lives with all that's going on in the world right now.”</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-b60000" name="module-b60000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><b>May 16:</b> “<a class="Link" href="https://www.choosechicago.com/event/jazz-zing-with-ava-logan/" target="_blank" >Ava Logan — Jazz Zing!</a>": 7 p.m. at the Haven Entertainment Center, 932 E. 43rd St., $30.</div> class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Members of the Beaus (from left: Kyle Hustedt, Kyle “Kiki” Russell and Olin Eargle) are pictured onstage. The “man-band” will perform a mash-up of pop hits and Broadway tunes during Chicago Cabaret Week.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Jantzen Loza</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h2>Mash-ups by a ‘man-band’ </h2><p>Cabaret performer Kyle Hustedt also stressed the need for escapism, especially as people have become increasingly addicted to scrolling on their phones.<b> </b></p><p>“People are socially starved, and they rely on these windows to other worlds that they don't actually get to participate in,” said Hustedt, a flower designer by day who owns Bukiety Floral on the Near West Side. “In a cabaret, you do. There's a different type of fulfillment that comes from a live performance in an intimate room.”</p><p>The<b> </b>Lake View<b> </b>resident creates engaging<b> </b>experiences as part of the Beaus, a “man-band” of professional singers that includes Kyle “Kiki” Russell, Olin Eargle, Dustin Lansbury and Justin Harnar. Along with special guests, they will bring a “<a class="Link" href="https://www.choosechicago.com/event/mashups-and-more/" target="_blank" ><u>Mashups and More</u></a>” show to Stars & Garters on May 12.</p><p>The setlist will include Broadway tunes and blended pop hits. For example, one medley combines One Republic’s “Love Runs Out,” Dolly Parton’s “9 to 5,” Gloria Estefan’s "Turn the Beat Around,” the “Will & Grace” theme song and RuPaul’s “Supermodel (You Better Work).” Another merges James Brown’s “I Got You (I Feel Good)" with Tommy James and the Shondells’ “Mony Mony” and “I Think We’re Alone Now.”</p><p>Hustedt said the group will ask the audience to vote on artists they want to hear before the show.</p><p>“This is making us all very uncomfortable, because we have to be up on so many songs,” he said, laughing. “But we thought it would also be tremendously exciting.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="InstagramUrl"> <blockquote </div> </div> </div></div><p>Hustedt formed the Beaus nearly a decade ago after studying opera at Northwestern University and transitioning into a career as a cruise ship singer. He formed a performance company called the Cabaret Project, and opened his own cabaret venue, the Monocle, in St. Louis. He even appeared in a Super Bowl commercial with Jennifer Hudson in 2015.</p><p>Through it all, the Beaus offered respite during the ups and downs of his career.</p><p>“The Beaus are a staple in my life,” he said. “We're there for each other, more than just comrades on the stage.”</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-620000" name="module-620000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><b>May 12:</b> “<a class="Link" href="https://www.choosechicago.com/event/mashups-and-more/" target="_blank" >The Beaus — Mashups and More</a>": 7 p.m. at Stars & Garters, 3914 N. Clark St., $30.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Erica Thompson

entries.title: DACA recipients in Chicago face uncertainty as renewal delays have them worried about being deported
entries.summary: <p>Melanny B. is about to celebrate a milestone.</p><p>But, instead of commemorating a master’s degree in higher education with family and friends, she’s planning to hide in the safest place she knows: her home.</p><p>Melanny, 31, came to the Chicago area from Ecuador when she was only 1 year old. She’s one of thousands of undocumented people who were brought to the United States as children and are awaiting their two-year renewals for the Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals, or DACA, program.</p><p>“In a time when I should be celebrating, in a time where this is a huge accomplishment, I am kind of left to be at home and safe and not put myself in jeopardy,” she told the Chicago Sun-Times, speaking on the condition she not be named because she fears for her safety.</p><p>DACA recipients, often referred to as "Dreamers," are seeing big delays, sometimes longer than six months, in renewals. Meantime, some have lost their jobs.</p><p>DACA recipients and immigration advocates view the delay as a way to force people out of the program that President Donald Trump has tried to stifle since his first term.</p><p>“Every day, I wake up with very much a lot of anxiety, a lot of depression," Melanny said. "My nervous system has just been really out of whack since November [2024]. And every day it’s something new, with DACA always being in limbo.</p><p>"It’s something that is just constantly being layered, and it is just such a heavy and tiresome weight to carry for the last 12 years of my life.”</p><p>According to U.S Citizenship and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration" >Immigration</a> Services, 80% of DACA renewals on average are taking four months to be completed. But there are unspecified processing holds being placed on applicants from certain countries, according to the National Immigration Law Center. Other delays are being blamed on biometric appointments, which had been paused during the pandemic.</p> <p>The pathway for undocumented people who were brought to the United States as children began under President Barack Obama in 2012, and there are now more than 830,000 people who have become Dreamers. In Illinois, there were 28,330 people with active DACA status as of June 2024, according to the National Immigration Forum.</p><p>Trump targeted the program during his first term, ending applications for new DACA recipients. There have been a plethora of legal battles, with a federal judge in 2021 continuing a prohibition of new DACA recipients. Along with the delays in renewals, new data provided to members of Congress shows U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement arrested 261 DACA recipients nationally between Jan. 1, 2025, and Nov. 19, 2025 — with 86 deported.</p><p>The latest blow to the program came last month, when the Justice Department’s Board of Immigration Appeals issued a decision that DACA authorization can’t protect recipients from removal proceedings — setting the stage for more deportations.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a alt="Damaris Bello (left), fought for her mother, Maria de Jesús Estrada Juárez, after she was deported to Mexico following a routine immigration check-in. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/96e0a5e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2100x1179+0+111/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe2%2Fb5%2Fd144fb684c738b93c6f533585c72%2Ftina-1.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/96e0a5e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2100x1179+0+111/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe2%2Fb5%2Fd144fb684c738b93c6f533585c72%2Ftina-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Damaris Bello (left), fought for her mother, Maria de Jesús Estrada Juárez, after she was deported to Mexico following a routine immigration check-in. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided </p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>‘My whole life is here’</h3><p>Within 24 hours, Maria de Jesús Estrada Juárez, 42, went from showing up to a routine immigration appointment in Sacramento, Calif., to having her feet and hands handcuffed and being loaded into a white van headed to Mexico.</p><p>Estrada Juárez, a DACA recipient, said federal immigration agents grabbed her finger to sign an electronic consent form against her will and that she she was told she was being deported based on a removal order from the year she came to the United States.</p><p>Estrada Juárez was picked up by family members and taken to Atlixco, a town in the state of Puebla, Mexico, where she hadn’t lived since she was 15.</p><p>Her mother served as her guide, telling her to avoid certain dangerous streets.</p><p>“It’s, like, I have to rebuild myself in another country that I don’t know," Estrada Juárez said. “Because, even though it's my birth country, do I feel like I belong in Mexico? I don’t.”</p><p>Estrada Juárez spent 40 days in Mexico, away from her 22-year-old daughter. They'd never been apart until then.</p><p>“It's always been just me and my daughter, and being away from her was, I was really devastated,” Estrada Juárez said. “There were some times where I found it really hard to breathe. I had to take a lot of deep breaths in order for me to be able to breathe again because just the idea of, God, I didn't know if I was going to be able to have a daily life with my daughter again. I felt like I was dead walking. She's my whole life.”</p><p>Estrada Juárez posted an emotional TikTok video pleading for help. Her daughter found legal help for her mother, and a federal judge ultimately ruled that her DACA status protected her from removal — and she was reunited with her daughter.</p><p>“When, when I was pregnant, I didn’t know if it was a boy. I didn’t know if she was a girl, but I did know that I had a lot of love for her, and I was anxious to meet my baby and hold my baby for the first time,” Estrada Juárez said. “And when I saw my daughter again at the border, I felt the same way. I feel like I held my daughter for the first time ever.”</p><p>Estrada Juárez immigration battle isn’t over. She is awaiting a decision on a motion to reconsider a green-card denial that was based on the same removal order issued when she was 15, which she said she was unaware of.</p><p>She can't work at her regular job as she tries to regain her status as a DACA recipient.</p><p>“If I have to start from the bottom, it wouldn't be the first time, and I will do it,” Estrada Juárez said. “But I have to, I have to do it the right way... Do I trust the system the same way? No. Am I going to keep trying to remain in a place where I call home? Yes. Because my home is my daughter, and my daughter's home is the U.S. I built the life here. My whole life is here.”</p><h3>‘Definition of justice’</h3><p>U.S. Sen. Dick Durbin said passing the Dream Act, which would allow undocumented people who were brought to the United States as children a path to legal citizenship, has been “at the top” of his list of priorities during his long career in the Senate. Durbin has introduced the Dream Act every Congress since 2001 and said he’s determined to give it one last try before retiring next January.</p><p>Durbin reintroduced legislation in December with co-sponsor Sen. Lisa Murkowski, R-Alaska.</p><p>“It’s real people who are counting on me to pass this legislation,” Durbin said. “Before I introduced the Dream Act, if you mentioned the Dreamers, people thought it was a 1960s rock 'n’ roll band. Now, it’s become a definition of justice when it comes to immigration. That young people who are here, through no design of their own, are given a chance to contribute to this country and have a future in the United States. </p><p>"I think public sentiment is stronger in support of Dreamers.”</p><p>Legislators are left to navigate protections for DACA and Dreamers — a broader term for undocumented people brought to the United States as children — as the Trump administration continues to send mixed signals. Last year, the federal Department of Homeland Security urged DACA recipients to self-depor.</p><p>"Illegal aliens who claim to be recipients of Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals are not automatically protected from deportations,” an agency spokeswoman told NPR.</p><p>That was even after Trump had this in 2024 of DACA recipients: “I want to be able to work something out.”</p><p>U.S. Rep. Delia Ramirez, D-Illinois, said calls have been coming in to her office from DACA recipients worried about their renewals. They’re being told to apply six months prior to their status expiring and that, in some cases, it’s taking longer, and they’re losing their jobs.</p><p>“They’re delaying, and I think they’re doing it on purpose because they’d like to see more DACA recipients lose status, so that makes them even more vulnerable for being targeted and deported,” Ramirez said.</p><p>She said she’s advising DACA recipients to speak with a legal expert, follow the rules and submit as early as possible.</p><p>“If they don’t submit, then they’re left completely out of status, and then, in that case, they’re now undocumented and vulnerable for deportation with no excuse from DHS,” said Ramirez, who points to contributions DACA recipients make to the United States, with Dreamers having paid more than $290 million in taxes and DACA recipients generating $2.1 billion for Social Security and Medicare. </p><p>“So, for those who don’t care about the morality, the individual person, the story about a 2-year-old coming with their family or unaccompanied young person, we should be asking ourselves the financial impact that a massive deportation campaign against Dreamers would have on the country as a whole and the impact that it would have especially for Dreamers that are in the healthcare industry,” Ramirez said.</p>
entries.author: Tina Sfondeles

entries.title: Pope Leo’s first year: How the Chicago native has stepped into the role
entries.summary: <p>A year ago Friday, Chicago-born Cardinal Robert Prevost became Pope Leo XIV — the first U.S.-born pontiff — and began his papacy with four words that would come to define much of his first year: “Peace be with you."</p><p>In Illinois, there was pride about his Chicago roots, excitement for his White Sox loyalty and eagerness to hear stories from friends and family who grew up with him.</p><p>As the months progressed, Pope Leo spoke on many issues, particularly regarding President Donald Trump's immigration crackdowns and, later, the war in Iran. </p><div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Pope Leo XIV celebrates Mass on Corpus Christi Day in St. John Latheran Archbasilica, in Rome, just weeks after becoming pontiff.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Andrew Medichini/Associated Press</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p><b>Elected as first U.S. pope </b></p><p>The election of Pope Leo XIV left Chicago abuzz for months .</p><p>Born in Chicago, raised in Dolton, Pope Leo, leaned into his Windy City roots from Day One.</p><p>There was speculation about restoring and/or reopening <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/architecture-design/2025/05/09/st-mary-of-the-assumption-can-its-connection-to-pope-leo-xiv-save-a-vacant-far-south-side-church" >his childhood church</a>, St. Mary of the Assumption on the Far South Side.</p><p>Almost a month into his papacy, the pope delivered his first address to a U.S. audience, via video link to a Mass held at Rate Field, home of the Sox.</p><p>“As you offer your own experience of joy and of hope, you can discover that you, too, are indeed beacons of hope,” the pope said to the 30,000 worshippers.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/architecture-design/2025/05/09/st-mary-of-the-assumption-can-its-connection-to-pope-leo-xiv-save-a-vacant-far-south-side-church" target="_blank" >Can Pope Leo XIV’s boyhood church — St. Mary of the Assumption — be saved?</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/pope-leo-xiv/2025/06/14/pope-leo-xiv-rate-field-mass-religion" target="_blank" >Pope Leo XIV delivers message of peace, unity at Rate Field in first address to his hometown </a> </li> </ul> </div> <p><b>Names Carlo Acutis first millennial saint</b></p><p>Furthering the plans of his predecessor, Pope Francis, Leo canonized the first millennial-era saint, Carlo Acutis, who died at 15 from leukemia.</p><p>Besides teaching catechism in a parish in Italy, Acutis had used his tech skills to create an online exhibit of more than 100 eucharistic miracles recognized by the church over many centuries.</p><p>Folks at Blessed Carlo Acutis Parish in Chicago, now renamed St. Carlo Acutis Parish, celebrated the official canonization in September. The Bucktown parish is the only one in North America named after the young saint.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/religion/2025/09/07/carlo-acutis-canonization-bucktown-parish-catholic-religion" target="_blank" >Bucktown parish honors namesake Carlo Acutis becoming first millennial saint after canonization by Pope Leo </a> </li> </ul> </div> <p><b>Speaks out against Operation Midway Blitz</b></p><p>During Trump’s <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration" target="_blank" >immigration crackdown in Chicago</a>, which began last September , the pontiff said he was troubled by the violent and at times "extremely disrespectful" ways migrants have been treated in the U.S.</p><p>"We have to look for ways of treating people humanely, treating people with the dignity that they have,” Leo told a group of reporters in November. “If people are in the United States illegally, there are ways to treat that. There are courts. There's a system of justice.”</p> <p>Gov. JB Pritzker and his wife, MK, met with the pope after those statements. The Pritzkers gave him a piece of artwork from an incarcerated immigrant woman as well as beers labeled “Da Pope.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/pope-leo-xiv/2025/11/19/jb-pritzker-pope-leo-xiv-vatican-meeting-operation-midway-blitz-chicago-beer" target="_blank" >Pritzker and Pope Leo XIV talk ‘Operation Midway Blitz’ and a Chicago beer honoring the pontiff </a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://www.npr.org/2025/11/18/g-s1-98318/pope-leo-calls-out-extremely-disrespectful-treatment-of-migrants-in-the-u-s" target="_blank" >Pope Leo calls out ‘extremely disrespectful’ treatment of migrants in the U.S. </a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/89c2f0a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6408x3596+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3f%2Fcc%2Fbf415b5a40acb0dfce66f785ef39%2F2219033106.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/baefe24/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6408x3596+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3f%2Fcc%2Fbf415b5a40acb0dfce66f785ef39%2F2219033106.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/89c2f0a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6408x3596+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3f%2Fcc%2Fbf415b5a40acb0dfce66f785ef39%2F2219033106.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Pope Leo XIV wears a White Sox cap as he meets newly wedded couples during the weekly general audience in St. Peter’s Square at the Vatican in June 2025.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Filippo Monteforte/Getty Images</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><p><b>Calls for peace abroad</b></p><p>During his first foreign trip of the papacy in late November, Leo championed for a two-state solution between Israel and Palestinians, saying it was the only way to resolve the conflict.</p><p>On the same trip, the Holy Father urged Turkey to act as a force for stability and peace amid the Israel-Hamas and Russia-Ukraine wars. Leo also traveled to Beirut and praised the Christian and Muslim coexistence in Lebanon.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/pope-leo-xiv/2025/11/30/pope-leo-xiv-doubles-down-on-insistence-for-2-state-solution-to-resolve-israeli-palestinian-conflict" target="_blank" >Pope Leo XIV doubles down on insistence for 2-state solution to resolve Israeli-Palestinian conflict </a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/pope-leo-xiv/2025/12/01/pope-leo-lebanon-asks-for-divine-gift-of-peace" target="_blank" >Pope in Lebanon asks for ‘divine gift of peace’ alongside country’s Christian and Muslim leaders </a> </li> </ul> </div> <p><b>Awards Nobel Prize of Theology to Riccardo Muti </b></p><p>Riccardo Muti, an Italian-born maestro, spent more than 10 years as Chicago Symphony Orchestra’s music director, becoming music director emeritus in 2023.</p><p>Pope Leo awarded Muti the Ratzinger Prize, also known as the Nobel Prize of Theology, both for his musical achievements and in celebration of Muti's friendship with Pope Benedict, who established the theology, philosophy and arts award in 2011.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/music/2025/12/02/pope-leo-riccardo-muti-ratzinger-prize-vatican-cso-benedict" target="_blank" >Pope Leo to recognize Riccardo Muti with esteemed award at Vatican this month</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p><b>Disagrees with Pritzker on assisted suicide bill </b></p><p>The pope said he was “very disappointed” that Illinois approved a law allowing medical assisted suicide after “explicitly” urging Pritzker to “respect the sacredness of life” and not sign the bill.</p><p>Pritzker said he signed the bill because he had been moved by stories of patients suffering from terminal illness .</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li >Pope disappointed by approval of Illinois’ assisted suicide legislation </a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure alt="This photo illustration created on April 13, 2026 shows a picture of US President Donald Trump on a screen and an AI-generated picture he posted on his Truth Social platform depicting himself as Jesus Christ after criticizing Pope Leo XIV. Trump later posted an AI-generated image seemingly depicting himself as Jesus Christ. In the image, the president appears dressed in red and white robes as he cures a man with his healing hand. The American flag is shown over his shoulder. Trump and the White House have previously shared AI-generated images, including one that showed the president dressed as the pope." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bed8f24/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8216x4611+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3f%2F5d%2Fc2f985c642d3baadd92474f2c8c0%2F2270717460.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/da3a815/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8216x4611+0+433/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3f%2F5d%2Fc2f985c642d3baadd92474f2c8c0%2F2270717460.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bed8f24/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8216x4611+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3f%2F5d%2Fc2f985c642d3baadd92474f2c8c0%2F2270717460.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>This photo illustration shows a picture of President Donald Trump and an AI-generated picture he posted on his Truth Social platform depicting himself as Jesus Christ after criticizing Pope Leo XIV. The White House later took the post down. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Mandel Ngan/Getty</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p><b>Feuds with Trump over war</b></p><p>Most recently the pontiff has gone toe-to -toe with Trump, continuing to speak out against the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran and calling for peace. </p><p>In April, Trump took to social media, writing that Leo was “WEAK on Crime, and terrible for Foreign Policy.”</p><p>The pope said the president’s threat to wipe out “a whole civilization” was “really not acceptable.”</p><p>After Trump posted a now-deleted AI-generated image depicting Trump as Jesus healing a sick man, Leo responded directly..</p><p>“I’m not afraid of the Trump administration or of speaking out loudly about the message of the Gospel, which is what the Church works for,” Leo said. “We are not politicians. We do not look at foreign policy from the same perspective that he may have.”</p><p>Trump later said the image portrayed himself as a doctor,</p><p>Pritzker posted a photo with Leo and wrote that “The Pope lifts his voice as part of a higher calling.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/pope-leo-xiv/2026/04/13/pope-leo-president-trump-attack-iran-war-chicago-catholics-reaction" target="_blank" >Pope Leo brushes off Trump attack as a Chicago Catholic leader says president is ‘waking up a sleeping giant’ </a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Zoe Singer

entries.title: Chicago-area woman finally gifted kidney transplant — by Sun-Times reader — after years of searching
entries.summary: <p>A year ago, Christine Hernandez and Eileen Kerlin Walsh were total strangers. Now, Hernandez is hoping to one day visit Kerlin Walsh’s family in Ireland.</p><p>Hernandez, 51, had spent the past eight years doing everything she could to find a kidney donor — from passing out business cards with her story to showcasing her search on billboards along highways. She had been among more than 3,700 people in Illinois who were waiting for a kidney transplant.</p><p>Kerlin Walsh read about Hernandez’s journey last fall in the Chicago Sun-Times. She was struck by the idea that Hernandez was searching for a miracle, and that Hernandez still held out hope despite the many setbacks she experienced.</p><p>“And I just thought, ‘What an opportunity, what a privilege to be somebody’s miracle,’” Kerlin Walsh said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-2d0001" name="image-2d0001"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Christine Hernandez speaks to a reporter in her living room." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ff540bd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9d%2F13%2F4ee77d5a4b6691d1285d9fe3a785%2Fkidney-09xx25-05.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ee6aa03/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9d%2F13%2F4ee77d5a4b6691d1285d9fe3a785%2Fkidney-09xx25-05.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ff540bd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9d%2F13%2F4ee77d5a4b6691d1285d9fe3a785%2Fkidney-09xx25-05.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Christine Hernandez had searched far and wide for years to find a living kidney donor.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Zubaer Khan/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Hernandez, now living with a new kidney thanks to a transplant in April, sees a connection with Kerlin Walsh akin to a “lifelong sisterhood.” People typically nickname their donated organ, and Hernandez decided to call her kidney “Miracle.”</p><p>“I didn’t know if I was ever going to have a chance to have a life again,” she said, “and to be able to enjoy life like I used to.”</p><h3>‘Held my own feet to the fire’</h3><p>Kerlin Walsh’s late mother had deeply believed in miracles.</p><p>Before her mother died in 2021, the two had gone on pilgrimages to healing apparition sites, spanning from Rome to the Knock Shrine in Ireland. The trips were a way for Kerlin Walsh to spend time with her mother because being one of 12 children meant she didn’t get very much alone time with her.</p><p>She thinks her mother played a role in her decision to become a kidney donor.</p><p>“I never felt that I lived up to her faith,” she said, tearing up. “Something about this … gives me the opportunity to maybe be my mother’s daughter.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-b60000" name="image-b60000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b947492/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F92%2F1f%2F99c1a2164139a5127b9436b9a8c3%2Fkidneyupdate-260506-02.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4f703f0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F92%2F1f%2F99c1a2164139a5127b9436b9a8c3%2Fkidneyupdate-260506-02.jpg data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/376ad42/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F92%2F1f%2F99c1a2164139a5127b9436b9a8c3%2Fkidneyupdate-260506-02.jpg" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ccd4fe7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F92%2F1f%2F99c1a2164139a5127b9436b9a8c3%2Fkidneyupdate-260506-02.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/376ad42/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F92%2F1f%2F99c1a2164139a5127b9436b9a8c3%2Fkidneyupdate-260506-02.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Eileen Kerlin Walsh holds a photo of her mother Mary Kerlin, whose lessons inspired her to donate her kidney.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Other factors convinced Kerlin Walsh to become Hernandez’s donor, too. They had the same blood type. Both were mothers to similarly aged children. And she read about Hernandez’s journey a month after turning 60.</p><p>The milestone made her reflect on her own life. She made a video about her accomplishments and about how she wanted to spend the next chapter of her life giving back.</p><p>“I watched it and rewatched it and really asked myself what I meant by that,” she said. “Held my own feet to the fire.”</p><h3>More challenges than finding a donor </h3><p>Kerlin Walsh reached out to UI Health in October to apply to become a kidney donor for Hernandez. She eventually received a letter in February confirming she was a match. She told her husband and children about her decision but held off from telling others.</p><p>Finding a donor was only one of Hernandez’s hurdles. She had a rare form of kidney disease known as MUC1. And she had extremely high levels of antibodies, which created a higher risk for her body to reject a kidney transplant.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" alt="Christine Hernandez gives a thumbs up while laying in a hospital bed." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9549685/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3024x1697+0+1167/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8c%2Fab%2F3a6fa30c4ec898bdbc7efe2f2b46%2Fkidney-260510-02.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fe85762/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3024x1697+0+1167/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8c%2Fab%2F3a6fa30c4ec898bdbc7efe2f2b46%2Fkidney-260510-02.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9549685/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3024x1697+0+1167/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8c%2Fab%2F3a6fa30c4ec898bdbc7efe2f2b46%2Fkidney-260510-02.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>After years of waiting for a kidney transplant, Christine Hernandez is recovering at home following a surgery in April.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The medical team at UI Health used a medication called Imlifidase that has helped patients like Hernandez successfully undergo a transplant by breaking down antibodies.</p><p>The drug has been approved for use by health authorities in Europe, the U.K. and Australia. But its maker, Swedish company Hansa Biopharma, is still in the process of getting approval through the U.S. Food and Drug Administration.</p><p>Dr. Lorenzo Gallon, director of the Abdominal Organ Transplant Program at UI Health, said it took about three months to get two vials of the medication through a process known as compassionate use. Gallon said he believes Hernandez is the first person in Illinois to get a transplant with Imlifidase.</p><h3>‘I just loved her instantly’ </h3><p>Kerlin Walsh was sitting in the waiting room of UI Health about a week before the transplant when she heard Hernandez’s name called. They hadn’t met before.</p><p>“This lady in front of me just walks up and away, and my heart was pounding,” Kerlin Walsh recalls. “And I thought, ‘Oh my God, oh my God, oh my God, that’s her. She’s right in front me. Should I say something to her?’”</p><p>She asked a nurse if donors and recipients typically meet, but the nurse said they don’t unless it happens by accident. Kerlin Walsh shared that she saw Hernandez in the waiting area. After talking to Hernandez, the nurse brought the women together.</p><p>Hernandez and Kerlin Walsh both remember hugging and crying. They talked about their families and exchanged phone numbers. The women started occasionally texting.</p><p>“I just loved her instantly, and I know she loved me, too,” Kerlin Walsh said.</p><p>Doctors administered one of the Imlifidase vials on April 9, and the concerning antibodies were no longer detected, Gallon said. That gave doctors a short window of time to perform the transplant.</p><p>Hernandez prayed with her children before the surgery, worried she wouldn't make it.</p><p>“It was scary,” she said. “You are going to go into this big surgery, knowing that I was high-risk. But I was going to go through with it. Just put all my faith in God and my doctors — I trusted them.”</p><p>Kerlin Walsh said reality didn't set in until about 10 minutes before the surgery.</p><p>“I just started sobbing because the fear really kicked in and then also the relief,” Kerlin Walsh said. “Because I didn’t want to leave the hospital that day not having made this donation.”</p><h3>‘I never lost my faith’</h3><p>Nearly a month after the surgery, Hernandez is back home in suburban River Grove. She’s wearing face masks to minimize exposure to anything that could derail her recovery, and she’s still going to the hospital every other day for checkups.</p><p>It could take another three to six months before she’s considered cleared. In a year, she can start to think of traveling outside of the United States, Hernandez said.</p><p>Dr. Gallon, of UI Health, said Hernandez is “almost out of the woods,” and they are closely monitoring her to make sure she doesn’t regress.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/88b28a7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3c%2Ff1%2F680838d24b25a4e474a8d4fdb5a5%2Fkidneyupdate-260506-14.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Eileen Kerlin Walsh, who donated her kidney to Christine Hernandez, is recovering at her home in Oak Lawn.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Kerlin Walsh is also back home in suburban Oak Lawn recovering from the surgery. The days after the surgery were a bit painful, but she was slowly starting to feel like herself. She took time off from work to recover, though she planned to spend some of that time working on her book.</p><p>“I just realized this was the final chapter of my book, and this was my final story of miracles and pilgrimages and faith and family and my tribute to my mother,” she said.</p><p>Hernandez is hopeful she will feel well enough one day soon to grab coffee with Kerlin Walsh and get to know her more. She wants to take a vacation with her children, maybe to visit family in Puerto Rico or Disneyland. She is eager to get back into advocacy, saying she wants to become a champion for others seeking organ donations.</p><p>“I feel like I have a calling to help people get out of this nightmare called dialysis,” she said.</p><p>Faith has also been a big part of Hernandez’s journey, and she thinks the positivity she maintained helped her after so many years of searching for a donor.</p><p>“I never lost my faith,” she said.</p><p>Hernandez’s family recently treated her to dinner at her favorite Peruvian restaurant to celebrate her birthday. One of her sons gifted her a framed painting depicting the moment when Kerlin Walsh checked in on Hernandez after the transplant. Both women are looking at each other, holding each other's hands.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a entries.author: Elvia Malagón

entries.title: Dolton community highlights positive change as one year anniversary of Pope Leo XIV's election approaches
entries.summary: <p>In the year since Cardinal Robert Prevost was named Pope Leo XIV, what used to be a typical block in Dolton has become a landmark drawing tourists from all over the world to his childhood home.</p><p>"It's definitely been a blessing," said Donna Sagna Davis, an eight-year Dolton resident who lives next door . "It's definitely been really great working with the tourists, it's been a breath of fresh air." </p><p>Residents who spoke to the Sun-Times Thursday say the changes in the neighborhood since Pope Leo's election have been mostly for the better. </p><p>Sagna Davis, 51, did her bit to help the tourists. Near her window, she set up a porta-potty, a container to leave prayer requests and a speaker playing sermons, all to encourage tourists to pray outside the home. The Village of Dolton purchased the home a couple of months after Pope Leo's inauguration.</p><p>"I can see the joy in everybody's eyes," Sagna Davis said as a group of four traveling from Vietnam posed for pictures in front of the Pope's old home. "They were really happy about this."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-fd0000" name="image-fd0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9389666/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6182x3469+0+326/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe3%2Fb6%2F552aa75a4c28a2becd21dea5d4b4%2Fpopedolton-260508-31.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/47a7acb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6182x3469+0+326/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe3%2Fb6%2F552aa75a4c28a2becd21dea5d4b4%2Fpopedolton-260508-31.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/cfe9e6d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6182x3469+0+326/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe3%2Fb6%2F552aa75a4c28a2becd21dea5d4b4%2Fpopedolton-260508-31.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Donna Sanga Davis and her son Jaden Davis, 18, stand in between their home and Pope Leo XIV’s childhood home in Dolton, Ill., on Thursday, May 7, 2026. Robert Prevost during their time feeding the hungry at Holy Rosary Church. </p><p>The added attention since Pope Leo's election attracted visitors closer to her age. </p><p>"A bunch of older people come here," said Nowling, 87.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-fb0000" class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Less than a mile north, construction workers with the Metropolitan Corporation used a tarp to cover holes and painted over graffiti at St. Mary's of the Assumption as former parishioners and community members look to restore the now-closed Riverdale church Pope Leo attended as a boy. </p><p>Scott Kuzminski, a former parishioner at the church with decades of family history, posted in a Facebook group about wanting to repair the church. Dan Dorman stepped up and offered to help with a free "band-aid fix." </p><p>"I think three things influenced my life. My parents, God and Dolton," said Dorman, owner of the Metropolitan Corporation. "I just felt like giving back."</p><p>Kuzminski wants to fully restore the church this year and gain landmark status to protect the building from condemnation and demolition. After its full restoration, he hopes the Archdiocese will reconsecrate the church so it can be used as a basilica — a Catholic church of historical significance granted special privileges by the pope. </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-410000" name="image-410000"></a> <picture class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A worker repairs part of the roof at the vacant St. Mary of the Assumption Roman Catholic Church on Thursday. The church Pope Leo attended as a boy has been vacant for years, and has deteriorated. The circular window behind the statue of the Blessed Virgin is broken. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Beyond hosting Mass and special events, Kuzminski wants the church to be a pillar for the community and be "respected just like Saint Sabina," referring to a South Side church led by Rev. Michael Pfleger, an activist priest. </p><p>"We cannot do this without the community's help and outreach," Kuzminski said. "They know this is a world thing," he added, but "they can take part in it too."</p><p>Kuzminski hopes people continue to contribute to the restoration "to celebrate this very modest church that gave the world the first American pope."</p><p>"I think that it's just gonna grow and grow. ... God willing, it will grow out to be a more peaceful area," Kuzminski said. </p><p>Sally Rock, who lived in Dolton for over 30 years, was back there Thursday, taking pictures and walking through the barren church recounting countless memories of services and religious milestones.</p><p>"I'm always drawn to come here because it just stirs up so many heartfelt memories," said Rock, 66. "My older brother was married here. There's a good chance that Pope Leo could have been an alter boy at my first communion." </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-5f0000" name="image-5f0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3a7c551/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6549x3675+0+345/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F47%2F4f%2F2543a23a4d0e913072e4a3b5da38%2Fpopedolton-260508-10.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ce0fe5b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6549x3675+0+345/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F47%2F4f%2F2543a23a4d0e913072e4a3b5da38%2Fpopedolton-260508-10.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/927cb8f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6549x3675+0+345/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F47%2F4f%2F2543a23a4d0e913072e4a3b5da38%2Fpopedolton-260508-10.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Scott Kuzminski, who used to be an altar boy at St. Mary of the Assumption Roman Catholic Church on the Far South Side, where Pope Leo XIV served as an altar boy when he was younger, stands inside the church, Thursday, May 7, 2026. Sagna Davis has received tickets and citations for her sermon-playing speakers and porta-potty. She believes those actions by the village infringe on her religious freedom. </p><p>It's definitely a growth for me and sometimes I'm faced with adversity," Sagna Davis said. "I'm trying to make sure everybody, when they come here, that they can use the bathroom or if they need some water or something, that it's available."</p><p>Kuzminski hopes one day Pope Leo will return to the church and home where he grew up.</p><p>"I know there's a whole lot of work to be done," Kuzminski said of the possibility. entries.title: No human remains found after search of South Elgin home
entries.summary: <p>No human remains were found at a South Elgin house that authorities searched Thursday while investigating the disappearance of a teenage girl.</p><p>Authorities did a forensic excavation at the house, on the 800 block of Revere Road, looking for evidence in connection with the disappearance of 17-year-old Kianna Galvin, who went missing in 2016.</p><p>The search yielded no human remains or additional investigative leads, according to a news release from Kane County State’s Attorney Jamie Mosser.</p><p>Ground-penetrating radar had detected anomalies beneath the surface that authorities determined merited further examination.</p><p>For more on this story, please visit <a class="Link" href="https://www.dailyherald.com/20260508/news/no-human-remains-found-after-search-of-south-elgin-home/" target="_blank" >DailyHerald.com</a>. </p>
entries.author: Susan Sarkauskas | Daily Herald

entries.title: Mayor Johnson, Chicago police gather for funeral honoring slain officer John Bartholomew
entries.summary: <p>Dozens of police officers and loved ones of slain Chicago police Officer John Bartholomew lined up outside of St. Andrews Greek Orthodox Church to say their final farewells Friday morning.</p><p>Bartholomew, 38, was shot and killed <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/04/25/officers-reportedly-injured-shooting-chicago-swedish-hospital-lincoln-square" target="_blank" >April 25 at Endeavor Health Swedish Hospital</a>, allegedly by a robbery suspect that he and his partner had brought there for an evaluation. The other officer was critically wounded. Bartholomew was a 10-year veteran of the Chicago Police Department.</p><p>Mayor Brandon Johnson, Chicago police Supt. Larry Snelling and other officers, wearing their dress blues and pins with Bartholomew's pictures on them, waited outside the church to welcome Bartholomew’s family and pay their respects at the funeral on Friday morning.</p><p>Hundreds of people gathered at the church on Thursday during a visitation.</p><p>Alphanso Talley, 26, is facing murder charges in connection with Bartholomew’s death. Prosecutors said that Talley told police he swallowed drugs and was taken to Swedish Hospital, where he had a firearm concealed under a hospital blanket. Prosecutors haven’t said how they believe Talley brought the gun into the hospital.</p><p>Later, when Talley was brought to a room and his handcuffs were removed for a CT scan, he pulled the gun from under the blanket and fired, striking Bartholomew and his partner, according to prosecutors.</p><p>Bartholomew was pronounced dead at Illinois Masonic Medical Center. The other officer was shot in his face. He has shown signs of improvement since the shooting, according to Fraternal Order of Police President John Catanzara, who also was at the visitation.</p><p><i>This is a developing story. Check back for updates.</i></p>
entries.author: Cindy Hernandez

entries.title: Blackhawks to sign acclaimed Russian forward prospect Roman Kantserov to NHL contract
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a> have convinced acclaimed Russian prospect Roman Kantserov to make the leap over to North America.</p><p>Kantserov's Russian club, Magnitogorsk Metallurg, confirmed Friday that they and Kantserov have terminated his contract in the KHL (Russia's top pro league) so he can sign his NHL entry-level contract with the Hawks.</p><p>The 2023 second-round pick has exploded into a star over the last two seasons, leading the KHL with 36 goals in 63 games this season.</p><p>He's a likely bet to star in the Hawks' top six next season, shortly after turning 22 in September. He could conceivably slot in on Connor Bedard's wing if Anton Frondell remains at center.</p><p>Kantserov is only 5-9, but he overcomes that lack of height with a lot of strength, a low center of gravity and a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2025/12/04/blackhawks-prospect-updates-roman-kantserov-john-mustard-sacha-boisvert-jack-pridham" target="_blank" >relentless drive</a>.</p><p>"He has the skill but also the bulldog mentality where he’s going to battle," Hawks assistant general manager Mark Eaton said in December. "He’s got a high motor. He’s not afraid to go to the tough areas and get to the front of the net. He does a great job getting his shot off and getting his shot through. He can get it off quickly. He’s had a decent amount of tip-in goals, as well."</p><p>"The tough areas...get even tougher when you get over to the NHL. The netfront areas, the walls, the corners — those will be a step up for him. But he’s proven all along he learns to adjust and find a way. I wouldn’t expect anything different at the NHL level."</p><p><i>This story will be updated.</i></p>
entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: Super Bowl champ, Barrington High alum Scotty Miller trying out for Bears
entries.summary: <p>Wide receiver Scotty Miller, the Barrington High School graduate who won a Super Bowl as a member of the 2020 Buccaneers, is trying out with the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> during their rookie minicamp this weekend.</p><p>Miller, who turns 29 on July 31, seemingly has an opportunity — Kalif Raymond is the only proven receiver the Bears added to team with Rome Odunze and Luther Burden. Miller has more than 11 catches in a season only once in the past five seasons, though. He caught nine balls for 62 yards in 13 games with the Steelers last year.</p><p>He spent his first four seasons with the Bucs, followed by one with the Falcons and two with the Steelers. He played only 27 special teams downs over the past two years.</p><p>He played for Bears receivers coach Antwaan Randle El, then a Bucs assistant, in 2020.</p><p>"There's some shared experiences," Bears coach Bears Johnson said Friday morning.</p><p>Also Friday, the Bears announced that they’d signed their bottom four draft picks — receiver Zavion Thomas, cornerback Malik Muhammad, linebacker Keyshaun Elliott and defensive lineman Jordan van den Berg — to standard four-year rookie contracts.</p><p>The Bears also announced the signing of 13 <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/25/bears-undrafted-free-agent-signing-tracker" target="_blank" >undrafted free agents</a>: Wyoming offensive lineman Caden Barnett, Kennesaw State running back Coleman Bennett, Tulane running back KC Eziomune, Georgia longsnapper Beau Gardner, Michigan State wide receiver Omari Kelly, Utah offensive lineman Jaren Kump, Iowa tight end Hayden Large, Wake Forest defensive lineman Jayden Loving, Louisville quarterback Miller Moss, Auburn offensive lineman Mason Murphy, San Diego State kicker Gabriel Plascencia, Oregon State defensive back Skyler Thomas and Florida State wide receiver Squirrel White.</p>
entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: U of I postpones finals, schools scramble following cyberattack on popular learning tool Canvas
entries.summary: <p>Many Illinois colleges and schools are scrambling after the online learning platform Canvas went offline Thursday following a cyberattack on its parent company.</p><p>The shutdown forced the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign to postpone all final exams and assignments scheduled for Friday, Saturday and Sunday, leaders told students in a message sent Thursday night.</p><p>Thousands of K-12 school districts and colleges use Canvas nationwide, including U of I and Northwestern University, to manage classes, post assignments and communicate with students.</p><p>Infrastructure, which runs Canvas, <a class="Link" href="https://status.instructure.com/incidents/9wm4knj2r64z" target="_blank" ><u>said last week</u></a> that it had experienced a “cybersecurity incident perpetrated by a criminal threat actor” that exposed the names, email addresses, student ID numbers of users, as well as internal messages. A hacking group <a class="Link" href="https://www.nytimes.com/2026/05/07/education/canvas-hacked-down-data-breach.html" target="_blank" ><u>claimed responsibility for the data breach</u></a>.</p><p>Northwestern University<a class="Link" href="https://northwesternuniversity.statuspage.io/incidents/wfythtrjf4lm" target="_blank" ><u> said Canvas remained offline</u></a> as of Friday morning and <a class="Link" href="https://www.it.northwestern.edu/about/news-events/2026/keep-teaching-without-canvas.html" target="_blank" ><u>provided suggestions</u></a> for professors to stay in touch with students and accept student work while it’s down.</p>
entries.author: Kalyn Belsha

entries.title: Woman receives kidney transplant from Sun-Times reader
entries.summary: <h3>Good morning, Chicago. ✶</h3><p>🔎 <b>Below</b>: After the Sun-Times covered one woman's long search for a kidney last fall, a reader stepped up to make a donation that would change both of their lives. </p><p>🗞️ <b>Plus</b>: Hundreds pay tribute to slain Chicago Police Officer John Bartholomew, Pope Leo XIV's first year and more news you need to know.</p><p>📝 <b>Keeping score</b>: The Cubs <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdidtit-l-j/" target="_blank" >continued their streak with an 8-3 win</a> over the Reds.</p><p>📧 <b>Subscribe</b>: <a Sheridan Road.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: Bartholomew, 38, was shot and killed <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdidtit-l-h/" target="_blank" >April 25 at Endeavor Health Swedish Hospital</a>, allegedly by a robbery suspect brought there by him and his partner for an evaluation. The other officer was critically wounded. Bartholomew was a 10-year veteran of the police department.</p><p><b>Key quote</b>: Among the public officials who attended Thursday was Ald. Matt O’Shea (19th), who called Bartholomew's death "another example, a reminder of just how unbelievably dangerous this job is for our first responders, our police officers."</p><p><a class="Link" >Woman is gifted kidney transplant by Sun-Times reader after years of searching</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdidtit-l-o/" target="_blank" >Elvia Malagón</a></h5><p><b>Consequential search</b>: Christine Hernandez, 51, spent the past eight years doing everything she could to find a kidney donor — from passing out business cards with her story to showcasing her search on billboards along highways. She had been among more than 3,700 people in Illinois waiting for a kidney transplant.</p><p><b>Generous reader</b>: Kerlin Walsh <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdidtit-l-b/" target="_blank" >read about Hernandez’s journey last fall in the Sun-Times</a>. She was struck by the idea that Hernandez was searching for a miracle and still held out hope despite many setbacks. "And I just thought, 'What an opportunity, what a privilege to be somebody’s miracle,'" Kerlin Walsh said.</p><p><b>'Lifelong sisterhood'</b>: Hernandez, now living with a new kidney after a transplant in April, sees a connection with Kerlin Walsh akin to a "lifelong sisterhood." People typically nickname their donated organ, and Hernandez decided to call her kidney "Miracle."</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdidtit-l-n/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>POPE LEO’S FIRST YEAR 🙏</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-b50000" name="image-b50000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a87028b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2818x1582+0+167/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb2%2F72%2F5cb216e247f6b36eedcf14418a14%2Fap26095379309459.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7d0cf18/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2818x1582+0+167/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb2%2F72%2F5cb216e247f6b36eedcf14418a14%2Fap26095379309459.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5876f8d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2818x1582+0+167/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb2%2F72%2F5cb216e247f6b36eedcf14418a14%2Fap26095379309459.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Pope Leo XIV addresses the faithful after delivering the Urbi et Orbi blessing - Pope Leo XIV stepped into the role, his hometown cheered him on</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdidtit-l-x/" target="_blank" >Zoe Singer</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdidtit-l-m/" target="_blank" >Mohammad Samra</a></h5><p><b>One year ago</b>: On May 8, 2025, Chicago native Cardinal Robert Prevost became Pope Leo XIV — the first U.S.-born pontiff — and began his papacy with four words that would define much of his first year: "Peace be with you." In Illinois, there was pride in his Chicago roots, excitement for his White Sox loyalty, and eagerness to hear stories from friends and family who grew up with him.</p><p><b>Looking back</b>: Pope Leo <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdidtit-l-c/" target="_blank" >spoke on many issues in his first year</a>, particularly President Donald Trump’s immigration enforcement tactics and, "It’s definitely been a blessing," said Donna Sagna Davis, an eight-year resident who lives next door.</p><p><b>Neighborhood impact</b>: Residents who spoke to the Sun-Times say changes in the
